literal: fashioning, measuring-out, world-forming power; often connected with √mā 'to measure, to form'
invariant_definition: *Māyā* has no single pan-Indian meaning. The thinnest common core is this: it names a power or condition through which manifest plurality is produced, sustained, or experienced. Beyond that, the term becomes school-specific. In Vedic and purāṇic usage it can mean divine skill, wondrous power, or formative capacity. In Advaita it becomes the cosmic register of *avidyā*, the principle by which the non-dual appears as manifold without undergoing real modification. In realist Vedāntas it names a real divine power, often identified with *prakṛti* or with the Lord's external energy. In Trika it is not illusion at all but a real self-contractive phase of Śiva's *svātantrya*, linked to the *kañcuka* analysis and the production of differentiated subjects and objects. The term therefore sits amid disputes over causality, *pariṇāma* versus *vivarta*, the status of the world, and the relation between God and manifestation. In practice *māyā* explains embodiment, limitation, and cosmic display, but what sort of explanation this is depends entirely on the metaphysics of the school using it.

per_school.definition: *Māyā* is the cosmic side of *avidyā*: beginningless, *bhāvarūpa*, and *anirvacanīya*, with *āvaraṇa* and *vikṣepa* functions. It is the *upādhi* of *Īśvara* and the explanatory principle for why one *brahman* appears as *jīva-jagat-Īśvara* without suffering real transformation. The world is therefore not *asat*; it is experienced, pragmatically ordered, and sublatable. Advaita uses *māyā* precisely to avoid both crude nihilism and real transformation of Brahman into the manifold.

*Māyā* is a real power of the Lord and is commonly identified with *prakṛti*, the non-conscious material principle that constitutes the body of the world. It is not an indeterminable illusion-principle but a real causal substrate ruled by Nārāyaṇa. Hence the same scriptural passages that Advaita reads as licensing *māyā-vāda* are read by Rāmānuja as describing God's actual creative power and the real emergence of the world. The polemic against *anirvacanīya māyā* is part of the broader defense of realist causality.

Madhva uses *māyā* for the Lord's real wondrous power, but the heavy doctrinal labor lies in attacking Advaita's *māyā-vāda*. The world is real, plurality is real, and God's creative agency is not an appearance masking a non-dual substrate. Dvaita therefore accepts a non-illusionist use of the term while rejecting the Advaitin claim that *māyā* is an ontological middle category. Its anti-Advaita arguments repeatedly press the point that what is experienced, scripturally taught, and causally effective cannot be dismissed as merely apparent.

*Māyā* is Kṛṣṇa's real *bahiraṅgā-śakti*, the external energy through which material embodiment, time, and karmic entanglement are administered. Gauḍīya authors commonly distinguish *guṇa-māyā*, the material complex structured by the three *guṇa*s, from the power that conditions the *jīva* for worldly existence. The point is not that the world is unreal, but that it is external to Kṛṣṇa's innermost *svarūpa-śakti* and therefore not the final register of divine life.

Bhāskara rejects *māyā* as an Advaitin illusion-principle and instead reads world-manifestation through real *pariṇāma*. The world is a genuine transformation of Brahman under real conditioning factors, not a merely apparent display. Accordingly, when scriptural language of divine making or measuring is read through *māyā*, it points to actual causal efficacy rather than indeterminable appearance. The term is acceptable only if detached from the Advaitin denial of real world-production.

*Māyā* is a real self-contractive phase of Śiva's *svātantrya*. It produces differentiated subjectivity and objectivity by imposing limiting conditions, often analyzed through the *kañcukas*. This is not an alien force set over against Śiva and not an illusion in the Advaitin sense. Rather, one consciousness freely takes on contracted forms and then recognizes itself through *pratyabhijñā*. The term therefore marks divine self-veiling and differentiation within a rigorously non-dual but non-illusionist ontology.

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'illusion.' That is seriously misleading. Most schools using *māyā* do not mean subjective illusion at all, and even Advaita does not mean simple unreality or *asat*. The word can import very different metaphysics: cosmic ignorance, real divine power, material nature, or Śiva's self-contraction. English should therefore signal the school-specific use instead of flattening everything into 'illusion.'
